issue_title: Potential file descriptor/memory leak in deis-logger
issue_body:
When deis-logger can't create or write to its log files, it seems to make runaway attempts to open the file numerous times per second. We have anecdotal evidence that this leads to high load and memory usage on the CoreOS host, leading to components crashing/restarting and having other oddities.

See https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/2372#issuecomment-61867924 for details.

issue_title: Restructure docs to have a Tutorials and Examples section (or two sections, for those things)
issue_body:
I think the current structure of the docs is not conducive to learning and easy access.

We should have a tutorial as part of the getting started component, and a suite of examples as an appendix.
It doesn't make a lot of sense for the `GlobusAuthorizer` docs to have a large example section embedded in the middle of it, and the structured code documentation generally seems mixed up in the middle of the high-level meta.

This is a clear separation that I'd like for us to enforce -- I think it will clean things up significantly.

issue_title: Unity snapshot - no way to configure multiple host_access
issue_body:
When attempting to set host access for a LUN snapshot, the `attach_to()` method will raise the following exception if there is already any existing host access configured for the snapshot -

```storops.exception.UnitySnapAlreadyPromotedException: The system was not able to promote a snapshot because it is already promoted. (Error Code:0x6000bdc)```

This reflects what is available in the Unisphere UI - once a snapshot is attached, the 'Attach to host' option is greyed out until it is first detached, after which new host access may be configured. As the method only accepts a single `host` argument, there is currently no way to configure multi-host access to a snapshot with storops, which the UI does support (as long as they're attached at the same time).

issue_title: Retraining and storing data
issue_body:
Currently we have a ton of encoding-specific data stored as constants all over the place.  This was done to mirror the C code initially, but I propose that we start diverging from the C code in a more substantial way than we have in the past.

The problems I see with the current approach are:

1.  Storing large amounts of data in code makes it much more difficult to read and separate out which files are just data from those that contain actual encoding/prober-specific code.
2.  Retraining the models we have (which are currently based on data from the late 90s) is difficult, because we would have to write a script that generates Python code. Yuck.
3.  It makes the barrier to entry for adding support for new encodings higher than it should be.  We should be able to have a tool that takes a bunch of text of a given encoding and generates the tables we need and determines things like the typical "positive ratio" (which is really the ratio of the token frequency of the 512 most common character types to the total number of tokens in a "typical" corpus) automatically.  The current layout of the code is very confusing to a new contributor (see point 1).
4.  Because retraining is difficult, chardet is going to get less accurate over time.  Speaking as an NLP researcher, I can confidently say that the genre of a text plays a big role in how likely certain character sequences are, and as time goes on the typical web text we see looks less and less like it did when Mozilla collected their original data.  Also, our accuracy for text that isn't from webpages is probably not that great.

So if we're agreement that the current approach is bad, how do we want to fix it?

I propose that we:

1.  Store the data in either JSON or YAML formats in the GitHub repository.  This would potentially allow us to share our data with chardet ports written in other languages (if they wanted to support our format).
2.  As part of the `setup.py install` process, convert the files to pickled dictionaries.
3.  Modify the prober initializers to take a path to either a pickled dictionary or a JSON/YAML file and load up that data at run-time.  Supporting both types of file would simplify development, since we could play around with models without having to constantly convert them to pickles.
4.  Modify `chardet.detect` to cache its `UniversalDetector` object so that we don't constantly create new prober objects and reload the pickles.

The only problem I see with this approach is that it will slow down `import chardet`, but loading pickles is usually pretty fast.

@sigmavirus24, what do you think?

issue_title: MRS codecs should use the Mrs() constructor
issue_body:
There's an asymmetry between the mrs() and eds() methods on ParseResults:

```python
>>> r = rest.parse('Dogs bark.', params={'mrs':'simple', 'eds':'native'})
>>> r.result(0).eds().to_dict()
{ ... }
>>> r.result(0).mrs().to_dict()
...
AttributeError: 'Xmrs' object has no attribute 'to_dict'
```

This is because the underlying codec for mrs(), simplemrs, instantiates Xmrs instead of Mrs(). Changing this should allow the ParseResult calls to be symmetric, and is probably the better thing to do anyway. Similarly, check that the other codecs instantiate their respective classes instead of the generic one.

```

Looking in `execute_search`,

```
    linked = conda.install.linked(prefix)
    extracted = set()
    for pkgs_dir in conda.config.pkgs_dirs:
        extracted.update(conda.install.extracted(pkgs_dir))  # <----- 99.99% time spent here

This `extracted` seems to be only used though to show the nice dots in the package list to mark the extracted packages. 

I, for one, would gladly trade the nice dots for 100x speedup, could it be somehow made configurable?

issue_title: list of used dependency versions for each build..
issue_body:
Hi,

we just discussed for bioconda the problem of updated runtime dependencies that break existing downstream packages (eg for perl etc..).

eg. think about the following situation: given a (perl) package A and a dependent package B, which only provides a minimal required version for A. Now A is updated and rebuild to a new version which drops the interface used by B. So the build of A is successful, but as soon as B is installed, the recent A is installed too and B fails on runtime.

Is is planned/possible/useful to add to each package also the *full list* of packages including version number used for the build?

This would very much ease 
- debugging for the described scenario and 
- enable the identification of outdated packages to be rebuild.

Thanks for feedback on that,
Martin

issue_title: Bug with uploading large files using `put_block_blob_from_path` and specifying content-md5
issue_body:
I believe there's a bug in uploading large files using `put_block_blob_from_path` and specifying md5 hash of the blob. What I noticed is that when uploading large block blobs, in the code 1st an empty block blob is created (line 1077) which I don't think is needed. Since a user is specifying content-md5 of the file and an empty blob is created by the SDK, their md5 hashes will not match and the user will get an error.

Please see this Stack Overflow thread for more details: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/34914261/python-azure-blob-storage-md5-check-fails-on-blob-upload-using-put-block-blob-fr.

issue_title: Attacks not working with PyTorch model
issue_body:
Most of the attacks are not working with the PyTorch model. The attack methods return a none type, when in fact they should return adversarial images. The only attack that has worked is the LBFGSAttack.

# Setup model
`model_conv = torchvision.models.resnet50(pretrained=True)`
`model_conv = model_conv.eval()`
`model = foolbox.models.PyTorchModel(model_conv, bounds=(0, 255),num_classes=1000, cuda=False)`

# Get image to perturb
`image, label = foolbox.utils.imagenet_example()`
`image = np.asarray(image.reshape([1,3,224,224]))[0]`
`label = np.argmax(model.predictions(image))`
`
# Attack criteria 
`criterion = foolbox.criteria.Misclassification()`
`attack = at.IterativeGradientAttack(model,criterion=criterion)`
`adversarial = attack(image, label)`

issue_title: People API not returning a good error if items is None
issue_body:
When I search by displayName or email, if my search is not successful, then the I get an error saying:

> 'items' object not found in JSON data: {'items': []}

However, I believe the library should handle the error with a response like:

> Search returned 0 results

I'm talking about these lines: https://github.com/CiscoDevNet/ciscosparkapi/blob/710aea82b2e227a23372d24c5f98653d32fece63/ciscosparkapi/api/people.py#L141-L144

I'm not sure if you want to handle that kind of events like I'm suggesting. If you want to do it like that, I can help with the code. If not, feel free to close this "issue".

issue_title: Preview presets
issue_body:
Predefined presets with tasks to deploy previews to make it easier to setup and mantain preview configuration for standard projects.

Should have a list of tasks and the "daemon task" and run preview tasks defined in frigg config after the preset tasks before running the daemon task.

Example 

```yaml
preview:
  preset: django
  tasks:
   - python manage.py loaddata fixtures/preview.json
```

issue_title: Improvements for manual mode
issue_body:
I'm currently in the beta program and i'm experimenting now with installing certs on some of my domains.
I try to automate all processes and almost all work nicely.
But there some probs and questions that i currently have.
My webserver is Lighttpd.
Standalone, Webserver, Apache, or any other auth mode is not usable for me because of my special server setup.

1. Auth requests through url "/.well-known/xxx" are OK but to force a specific content-type is a problem because this file simply don't have a file extension. I don't want to change the default mime type for all not explicitely listed extensions to "application/jose+json". Currently i have to write proxy scripts for all that auth urls to change the mime type. There are 2 possible fixes for that. Change auth request url to a specific file extension url like `/.well-known/xxx.josejson` than we can assign mime types to it or disable the requirement for the specific content-type for auth request.
2. Is it recommended to create one cert for all domains or creating one cert for one domain? Currently i create one cert for one domain. I have many domains, will this be a problem in the future?
3. Add the possibility to specify a output file for the auth request string. Currently there is no chance to fully automate the manual mode. I've hardcoded it locally in "plugins/manual.py" to store that stuff to a file.
4. Add the possibility to disable the "Hit Enter to proceed" after the notification message for the request token. In combination with my point 3 this will result in a fully automated process.
5. Add possibility to concat public and private key to one file. For example lighttpd requires that.
6. I'm runnning into "RateLimited for domain xxx" because i've done some tests. When will this limit be reseted?

Locally i've now a working solution for a full automated process in manual mode. I guess this will be also helpful for outer people.
Here is my workflow:
1. Run `letsencrypt-auto --renew-by-default --agree-dev-preview --server https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory auth -a manual -d xxxx.com`
2. I've changed my manual mode to save the auth token file on disk and skipping the waiting message.
3. My domain serves the auth file automatically with correct mime types (Requires a big workaround, more info see Point 1 above)
4. Letsencrypt will successfully finish the auth for new certs and renewals.

issue_title: Timeout during sync resulting in sync not closing
issue_body:
While testing the sync functionality, I decided to turn my VPN off mid sync. After a good while (at least 10min?) uploads managed to continue. A while later, I turned on VPN again, and uploads again stopped for quite a while. However, a while later I got to errors:

```
b2_upload(/mnt/data/borgtest/data/0/249, data/0/249, 1470290017627): UnknownError() unknown error: ReadTimeout(ReadTimeoutError("HTTPSConnectionPool(host='pod-000-1005-09.backblaze.com', port=443): Read timed out. (read timeout=None)",),)
b2_upload(/mnt/data/borgtest/data/0/250, data/0/250, 1470290019022): UnknownError() unknown error: ReadTimeout(ReadTimeoutError("HTTPSConnectionPool(host='pod-000-1009-00.backblaze.com', port=443): Read timed out. (read timeout=None)",),)
```
Those two files may have been uploading when I turned VPN on, but I imagine others would have been as well.

Anyhow, after the sync finished uploading all other files, the sync progress remained stuck at 289/293, (this seems to be a bug as well, I'm pretty sure 291 files managed to upload fine) and did nothing for at least 40min after which I killed it.

Running `b2 sync` again it happily uploaded the remaining two(!) files.

TL:DR
- Upload timeouts very slowly (may be ok) but doesn't seem to retry if it fails
- Progress status may report faulty uploads when there have been Errors.

issue_title: fp.min/fp.max/fp.neg Bug with +/- 0.0
issue_body:
On the `master` branch at commit `cef8c67cc0e6b1b024d8e4e0150b2020688de7c7` with a debug + tracing build, consider the following input:

```
(set-logic QF_FP)
(assert (= (_ -zero 2 3)
                (fp.max       ; Point 4
                  (fp.min     ; Point 3
                    (fp.neg   ; Point 2
                      (fp.min ; Point 1
                        (_ -zero 2 3)
                        (_ +zero 2 3)))
                    (_ +zero 2 3))
                  (fp #b1 #b10 #b01))))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```

If this is saved into `test.smt2` and then run with `z3 -smt2 test.smt2`, Z3 reports `unsat`.  Tracing the logic of this, at `Point 1` +/- 0.0 is possible based on the reasoning in issue #68, and similarly both +/- 0.0 is possible at `Point 2`.  `Point 3` still allows +/- 0.0, either of which is greater than `(fp #b1 #b10 #b01)`, allowing `Point 4` to be +/- 0.0.  Following this reasoning, this input should be `sat` at the comparison to -0.0.

While it might be possible to minimize this further, I found that the `fp.neg` is significant, as are nested uses of `fp.min` and `fp.max`.

This might be a duplicate of the closed issue #68.  I'm reporting it separately here as opposed to reopening #68 as this issue seems intertwined with `fp.neg` in some way, which is distinct from #68.

issue_title: More flexible DenseLayer
issue_body:
A common use case for a dense layer is to stack it on top of a recurrent layer to compute a prediction at every time step. Since the recurrent layer output is of shape `(batchsize, seq_len, feats)`, this requires three layers: A `ReshapeLayer(..., (-1, feats))`, the `DenseLayer(..., num_units)`, and possibly another `ReshapeLayer(..., (batchsize, seq_len, num_units))`. The latter requires either the `batchsize` or `seq_len` to be known at compile time, or obtained as symbolic variables.

The operation inside the `DenseLayer` is quite simple: It consists of `T.flatten()` followed by `T.dot()`.
Now `T.dot(x, y)` already supports arbitrary dimensionality for `x`, performing the dot product over the last axis only. The only reason we cannot directly use it on the output of a recurrent layer is that it flattens the input to be a matrix, in this case of shape `(batchsize, seq_len * feats)`.

We could make the `DenseLayer` much more flexible simply by allowing to specify the number of axes to be flattened (or to be kept). I propose to add a keyword argument with the following semantics:
* If it's a positive integer, this gives the number of axes not to be flattened. E.g., setting it to `1` (the default) means to keep the first axis and flatten the remaining ones. Put differently, it gives the index of the axis all following ones are to be collapsed under.
* If it's a negative integer, it also gives the index of the axis all following ones are to be collapsed under, counted from the end as is customary in Python for negative indices. E.g., setting it to `-2` means to flatten the last two axes, and `-1` means to only flatten the last axis, which does nothing.
* If it's zero, we throw an error because we think this is never intended and based on a misunderstanding? Otherwise, this would flatten all the input into a single vector, losing the batch dimension on the way.

I'm not too sure about the name of the keyword argument, though. It could be `keep_axes`, or `distribute_over` (as the dot product is distributed over the given number of axes).

Alternatively, we could also have a full-blown `shared_axes` argument listing the axes to distribute the dot product over, but this would involve moving all the others to the end before the dot product and restoring the order afterwards (e.g., a `(3,4,5,6)` tensor with `shared_axes=(0,-1)` would have a `(20,num_units)` weight matrix and produce a `(3,num_units,6)` output) -- maybe that's too much flexibility.

issue_title: CFGAccurate does not handle ret to grandparent function
issue_body:
For example, consider the following code:

```
qux:
  call bar

bar:
  mov rax, rsp
  call foo

foo: 
  mov rsp, rax
  ret
```

In this case, `foo` returns directly to `qux`, skipping `bar`. Right now, `bar` is marking as non-returning which makes the analysis think that the `call bar` never returns which is wrong.

A special case of this frequently happens when dealing with architectures where `call` is used to reserve space on the stack. For example, code like this is common in AVR:

```
            0x00000c1e      cf93           push yL
            0x00000c20      df93           push yH
            0x00000c22      00d0           rcall 0xc24                 ;[1]
            0x00000c24      00d0           rcall 0xc26                 ;[2]
```

These calls are never returned to, they just reserve space on the stack.
Right now, CFGAccurate will create a function for `0xc1e`, `0xc24` and `0xc26`, and the first two will be marked as "not returning".

 An alternative to dealing with this would be to rely on special-casing of all such idioms to never get us in the situation where we have separate functions for "fake" calls. Or is it worth to handle this using the callstack? We should be able to figure out if a `ret` goes to a location farther up in the callstack than the parent, would this perhaps also be useful information for eliminating these fake calls?

issue_title: Possibly argument of wrong type is used to initialize SimStruct
issue_body:
According to the implementation of `SimStruct`, it seems that the `fields` attribute is expected to be an instance of `dict`. However, in at least the `glibc.py` module, there are lots of `SimStruct`s that are initialized with fields argument of type `list`, for example:

```python
# struct group * getgrnam (const char *NAME);
"getgrnam": SimTypeFunction([SimTypePointer(SimTypeChar(label=None), label=None, offset=0)], SimTypePointer(SimStruct([], name="group", pack=True), label=None, offset=0), label=None),
```

This could lead to a confusing "Can't fit value into a return value" error:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
  File "/home/user/angr/angr/sim_procedure.py", line 310, in ret
    arg_types=[False]*self.num_args if self.cc.args is None else None)
  File "/home/user/angr/angr/calling_conventions.py", line 556, in teardown_callsite
    self.set_return_val(state, return_val)
  File "/home/user/angr/angr/calling_conventions.py", line 608, in set_return_val
    raise ValueError("Can't fit value %s into a return value" % repr(val))
ValueError: Can't fit value <BV32 unconstrained_ret_getgrnam_387_32{UNINITIALIZED}> into a return value
```

The real cause of that problem is that the `SimType.fields` was initialized with a `list` instead of `dict`, hence the following code threw an AttributeError:

```python
class SimStruct(SimType):
    # ...
    def _with_arch(self, arch):
        if arch.name in self._arch_memo:
            return self._arch_memo[arch.name]

        out = SimStruct(None, name=self.name, pack=self._pack, align=self._align)
        out._arch = arch
        self._arch_memo[arch.name] = out
        # Next line will raise AttributeError("'list' object has no attribute 'iteritems'") in case
        # the self.fields were initialized with 'list' instead of 'dict'
        out.fields = OrderedDict((k, v.with_arch(arch)) for k, v in self.fields.iteritems())
        return out
```

Which will be later caught by:

```python
class ArgSession(object):
    # ...
        try:
            dbg.settrace('unconstrained_ret_getgrnam_387_32' in val.variables)
            betterval = self._standardize_value(val, ty, state, None)
        except AttributeError:
            raise ValueError("Can't fit value %s into a return value" % repr(val))
```

To this time, I was unable to fix it by myself, 'cause I'm not familiar with SimType at all. Or am I missing something?

Would people be objecting to introducing a ProxyError exception or even one called UserError?

This would allow us to raise exceptions which are defined as being due to a typo or mistake or bad code entered at the IPython prompt, rather than a bug in Ganga.

I'd like to make the prompt a little more friendly as some people associate the trace with something going horribly wrong rather than the trace being there to help them.

issue_title: LibRealSense vs Win-DCM-RealSense distortion model
issue_body:
We are using SR300.

The distortion parameters returned by librealsense's rs_get_stream_intrinsics() ("Inverse Brown Conrady" model) are different from the parameters returned by the Windows DCM driver. They are not the same, nor do the DCM parameters match the "Forward Brown Conrady" parameters.

I suppose this is intentional and they use different distortion models? This was implied by **Wollimayer** in [Issue 46](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/46).

Is there a way to retrieve the parameters of the Windows DCM distortion model using librealsense? Or maybe there's a known function that translates between the models? 
We want to substitute code that uses these parameters and that depends on the DCM with librealsense.

**Note 1:** To the best of our knowledge, the DCM distortion model is the same as the OpenCV distortion model, which is also the same as the Matlab Computer Vision Toolbox distortion model. Seems like someone might have dealt with these parameter translations before.

**Note 2:** We do get the same 3x3 projection matrix in both APIs.

**Note 3:** We tested with two SR300, one is an older development model and one is a commercial model from Creative.

| Camera Model |  SR300  | 
| Firmware Version |  3.21.0.0 |

issue_title: Understanding multi iteration update of model in PPO
issue_body:
I have a general question about the updating of the network/model in the PPO algorithm. If I understand it correctly there are multiple iterations of updates done on the model weights with data that is created from the environment (with the model before the update). Now I think that the updates of the model weights are not correct anymore after the first iteration because the model weights changed and therefore the training data is outdated (since the model would now give different actions in the environment and therefore different rewards). 

So is this some form of approximation or augmentation on the data by using the data that was created by an older model for multiple iterations or am I missing something here? And if yes where was this idea first introduced or better described? And where is a (emperical) proof that this still leads to a correct weight updating?

I am new to this field so I am sorry if this is an obvious question.

issue_title: HTTPS enabled but django admin EmbedVideoField is embedding unsecure HTTP
issue_body:
It seems in the admin the form still tries to embed an HTTP video even if HTTPS is enabled and the request to page is done via HTTPS. Seems all video backends are doing this. Inspecting values in the `backend.protocol()` showed `allow_https=True` and `is_secure=False` which gets used when generating the url.

When I intercepted the actual request, django's `request.is_secure()` returns True. Seems this should be overriding the default 'False' value somehow.

The request context processor is present as well.

issue_title: Scalar graphics: x axis doesn't represent global_step values
issue_body:
Hi! 
I've got strange behavior of scalar graphics. 
The X axis doesn't represent `global_step` values, which are set in `FileWriter.add_summary()` method.

**Here's a small example (from Jupyter Notebook):**

```
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

!rm ./test/*
tf.reset_default_graph()

pl = tf.placeholder(tf.int64, (), 'pl')
res = tf.add(pl, 1)
tf.summary.scalar('res', res)

summary = tf.summary.merge_all()

writer = tf.summary.FileWriter('./test', graph=tf.get_default_graph())        
with tf.Session() as sess:
    sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())

    for i in range(100):
        _res, _summary = sess.run([res, summary],
                                   feed_dict={pl:i})
        writer.add_summary(_summary, global_step=i)

!tensorboard --logdir ./test/ 
```

issue_title: Processing advertisments on parent migration
issue_body:
Looking at the current implementation I see that when a child receives
an advertisement from its parent, which indicates that the parent has changed
its partition, it updates the leader information. However, it looks to me that the 
Router Id Sequence number isn't updated.

This is problematic, since it might happen that the Router Id Sequence in 
the new partition is lower that in the former one. This means that the routing
information sent in consecutive advertisements won't be processed. If the child
is a REED then this will cause the node to think that it doesn't have connectivity
to the leader and in effect leave the partition.

Is that an intended behavior?

issue_title: Data race with new prometheus aggregation
issue_body:
Talisker 0.9.14 introduced a workaround for a memory leak in prometheus_client's multiprocess support (see https://github.com/prometheus/client_python/issues/275)

This workaround relied on the *child_exit* server hook, and the assumption that signal handling was serialized by the gunicorn main process. This turned out to to be true for every signal except SIGCHLD, which is the one that fires *child_exit*.

When you have large numbers of workers, this results in multiple SIGCHLDs being sent to the master process, and later signals can interrupt a running *child_exit*. Talisker did not protect against this, which could result in some data loss.  Additionally, because *child_exit* is run in a signal handler context, it cannot safely do any IO, which was causing some additional issues. 

#296 attempts to fix this issue, by doing as little as possible in child_exit, and moving the handling into the main gunicorn master loop, which seems to fix the problem.

issue_title: Fix assumptions around 'mode' for the pydatalab airflow operators
issue_body:
LoadOperator (table is always specified since it's a required parameter):
 - table does not exist in BQ: We should create the table. In this case, the value of mode should not matter. Perhaps we could ensure that it's not 'create' and is either 'append' or 'overwrite' because should this operator fail after table creation and re-execute due to a pipeline retry, then we would need this to not fail because of trying to create a table that existed.
 - table exists in BQ: We need to know whether we should append or overwrite. Hence from a parameter validation standpoint, we should throw if mode is 'create. An argument can be made that it's always safe to append in this case, but this choice should be the user's. 

Note: From the above, it's clear that mode is never 'create' and should always be one of 'append' or 'overwrite'. 

ExecuteOperator (table is an optional parameter): 
 - table is specified: We need to know whether we should append or overwrite. From a parameter validation perspective, we should throw if mode is anything else.
 - table is not specified: BQ will create a new temporary table for the query results. We should throw if mode is _not_ None, since it doesn't make sense for a mode to be specified when a table is not.

issue_title: `plot_by_id` doesn't handle identical 2D setpoints
issue_body:
When using `plot_by_id` a non-descriptive error is raised when identical setpoints are provided. Although it is stupid to try to plot something as a function of `[1, 1, 1]` and `[0, 0, 0]`, we should handle that stupidity properly. And a single point in 2D space ought to work.

### Steps to reproduce

```python
import numpy as np
import qcodes as qc
from qcodes.dataset.measurements import Measurement
from qcodes.dataset.plotting import plot_by_id
from qcodes.tests.instrument_mocks import DummyInstrument

dac = DummyInstrument('dac', gates=['ch1', 'ch2'])
dmm = DummyInstrument('dmm', gates=['v1', 'v2'])

meas = Measurement()
meas.register_parameter(dac.ch1)  # register the first independent parameter
meas.register_parameter(dac.ch2)  # register the second independent parameter
meas.register_parameter(dmm.v1, setpoints=(dac.ch1, dac.ch2))  # now register the dependent oone

with meas.run() as datasaver:
    for _ in range(20):
        datasaver.add_result((dac.ch1, 1),
                     (dac.ch2, 2),
                     (dmm.v1, 0))

run_id = datasaver.run_id

ax, cbax = plot_by_id(run_id)
```

### Expected behaviour

issue_title: Cannot disable automatic rendering of Meta.fields, even when render_unmentioned_fields is False
issue_body:
My (somewhat strange) use case involves having a ModelForm with only a subset of a model's fields.

Now, the helper layout is generated at runtime depending on some dynamic conditions, and might not contain all fields in Meta.fields. However, crispy likes to always render missing fields at the end.

Why is this the enforced behavior, is there any danger in not rendering all fields, but have them appear in Meta.fields?

My current solution monkey patches `FormHelper.render_layout` to avoid this, but I can provide a pull request if you think this is worth being added to core django-crispy-forms using some form of settings flag.

issue_title: Twitch Drops
issue_body:
### Checklist

- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [x] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [x] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description

Same question as in https://github.com/chrippa/livestreamer/issues/1629

"Twitch Drops" are rewards randomly given to people watching live streams of games while logged on Twitch. Since Streamlink already supports Twitch authentication, a support for Twitch Drops would minimize inconvenience for Twitch Drop idlers, as using only the audio would minimize bandwidth usage.

I would expect to be able to receive a Twitch Drop (e.g. for _The Elder Scrolls Legends_) if I'm _watching_ a stream in audio only mode with Streamlink. If this is not the case, what could be done to support Twitch drops? Otherwise, maybe this could be documented, at least with the Github issue right here.

### Expected / Actual behavior

Expected behavior: Twitch drops are awarded to people using Streamlink.

Actual behavior: unknown.

I will test the Twitch drops in the afternoon, but the random aspect of Twitch drops makes it hard to test the support of Twitch drops. This question has not been asked or documented yet, based on my search for Github issues and Streamlink documentation.

### Reproduction steps / Explicit stream URLs to test

1. Create the authentication token
2. Create the config file `%APPDATA%\streamlink\streamlinkrc` and paste the token inside it.
3. Stream the audio only: `streamlink twitch.tv/justalazygamer audio_only`
4. Wait for a Twitch Drop.

### Environment details

Operating system and version: Windows 10 64 bits
Streamlink and Python version: 0.8.1 and 3.6.1 respectively.

issue_title: [Feature Request] Option to play/output audio only from a video stream
issue_body:
----

### Checklist

- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [x] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [ x] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description

I think a great additional feature would be to add an option to only play the audio track from a given stream. This would be a great option for someone who just wants to listen to say, a sports game, but he's on low bandwidth so he doesn't want to waste it on a video stream that he doesn't need. Of course, we can always use the `worst` option now, but that still requires playing video. Or maybe someone wants to watch a stream in 1080p, but in a different language that's only available with a 480p stream. With audio only, they could play the two side-by-side without wasting the additional resources on playing the video from the lower-quality stream. VLC or ffmpeg always recognizes two distinct streams (audio and video), so that might make it easier to only send the one stream to the player. This could be simply implemented in command line with a parameter like `--audio-only`.

```

As expected, whenever I click back and forth on the slider, the function `on_value_change` is called and the current value is printed on the output. However, when I launch the animation via the play button, nothing is printed. In fact, it looks like `on_value_change` is not even called. Could anyone please explain the reason? Is it a bug?

issue_title: Python
issue_body:
One of the issues I have come across is that in python, arguments are initialized when function is defined not during each execution leading to issue in case argument is mutable e.x. list or dictionary. In that case it behaves like a static variable existing over all call to that function. 

>>> def foo(counter=[0]):
...   counter[0] += 1
...   print("Counter is %i." % counter[0]);
... 
>>> foo()
Counter is 1.
>>> foo()
Counter is 2.
>>>

issue_title: pypi version requires protobuf 3.4
issue_body:
Because `sdist` includes `onnx.pb.h`, the generated source distribution can only be used with the same(ish) version of `protobuf` as whoever generated the source. Particularly, `pip install onnx` doesn't work right now if you have protobuf 3.5.

Since you need protobuf at compile time anyway, it'd probably be better to change `setup.py` to not include these files in `sdist`s but generate them at compile time instead....

issue_title: Subprocess().execute: no exit code on correct exit (unstable)
issue_body:
Sometimes on fast command exit we collect stdout, but can not collect exit_code.
Example reproduce:
```python
for _ in range(60*60*8): runner.check_call('echo aaa', timeout=1)
```
During iteration, normally not on the start, we will have:
```
DEBUG:exec_helpers.subprocess_runner:aaa
DEBUG:exec_helpers.subprocess_runner:Command 'echo aaa' exit code: EX_OK<0(0x00)>
DEBUG:exec_helpers.subprocess_runner:Executing command:
'echo aaa'

Traceback (most recent call last):...
```

issue_title: toPython() doesn't convert datetimes with millis
issue_body:
ed.summers, 2009-02-17T10:55:06.000Z 
It's nice that toPython() will convert dateTime XSD datatypes to datetime python objects:

>>> from rdflib import Literal , Namespace
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> XSD_NS = Namespace(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#')
>>> Literal('2008-02-09T10:46:29', datatype=XSD_NS.dateTime).toPython()
datetime.datetime(2008, 2, 9, 10, 46, 29)

But if you add milliseconds it doesn't seem to work:

>>> Literal('2009-02-17T05:48:53.203307', datatype=XSD_NS.dateTime).toPython()
rdflib.Literal('2009-02-17T05:48:53.203307', language=None, 
datatype=rdflib.URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#dateTime'))

This bit me recently when I couldn't figure out why adding triples with datetime.now() wasn't 
working as expected:

>>> Literal(datetime.now()).toPython()
rdflib.Literal('2009-02-17T05:52:13.612998', language=None, 
datatype=rdflib.URIRef('http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#dateTime'))

It appears that RDFAlchemy had to hack around this problem: 
http://www.openvest.com/trac/wiki/CustomizingLiterals
